summary: Stancil argues that modern databases and analytics tooling have become extraordinarily powerful, yet the practical business impact of data teams has often been underwhelming because the underlying data lacks the “potential energy” to answer real questions. He suggests that the next leap won’t come from better analysis on structured tables, but from new database-like systems that can natively work over unstructured documents and LLM-driven workflows.
money_quotes: In other words, most databases are very good, and most data teams are still a disappointment.
The data of a mid-sized B2B SaaS product simply doesn’t have the potential energy of Google’s search histories, or of Amazon’s browsing logs.
Behavioral data isn’t “truth;” it’s an observable proxy, the input to a kind of analytical alchemy that attempts to turn individual outcomes into generalizations about intentions.
The potential energy in analytics is in a better data source, not in better analysis.
And it’s not the analyst that needs to be different, but the database.
key_insight: Analytics’ next breakthrough depends less on smarter analysis and more on rethinking the database to handle unstructured, LLM-accessed knowledge as the primary data source.
